pred_reason: SWC-101 applies because the constructor performs exponentiation without bounds checking, allowing integer overflow when _priceMultiplierDecimals is too large. This matches the classic integer overflow vulnerability pattern where arithmetic operations exceed type limits. Evidence: Constructor calculates priceMultiplier = 10 ** _priceMultiplierDecimals without overflow protection; Overflow occurs when _priceMultiplierDecimals >= 78, corrupting price calculations; Vulnerability follows SWC-101 integer overflow definition: unchecked arithmetic operation exceeding type maximum

findings.Description: The constructor calculates priceMultiplier = 10 ** _priceMultiplierDecimals without bounds checking, allowing overflow when _priceMultiplierDecimals >= 78. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Contract deployment with large exponent values causes priceMultiplier overflow, leading to incorrect price calculations (likely returning 0) and complete breakdown of oracle functionality

findings.Issue: Negative-to-unsigned conversion risk in oracle response
findings.Description: The read() function directly converts oracle.latestAnswer() (int256) to uint256 without checking for negative values, causing reverts when oracle returns negative prices. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Oracle becomes temporarily unusable when negative prices occur, disrupting all dependent protocols and potentially causing financial losses due to failed time-sensitive operations
findings.Location: ChainlinkOracleAdapterV2.read() function, line: uint256 oracleOutput = uint256(oracle.latestAnswer())
